skill.name: error-debugging-error-analysis
skill.description: Guides systematic debugging, root-cause analysis, incident response, and observability improvements for application and distributed system errors.
skill.summary: Analyze errors, trace root causes, and plan reliable fixes for production and development incidents.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review found they are Markdown documentation examples rather than executable skill code. Network and environment-variable references are observability examples, and no prompt-injection attempt or malicious exfiltration path was found.

low_findings.title: Static code execution findings are documentation false positives
low_findings.description: Backticks and code fences in SKILL.md and the playbook were flagged as shell execution. They are Markdown references and examples, not executable skill scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown formatting or illustrative text. I found no executable file or instruction that runs these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Network calls are illustrative observability examples
low_findings.description: The axios, fetch, runbook, dashboard, and status page references are examples inside a debugging playbook. They do not create hidden network behavior in the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references are embedded in code samples or incident templates. There is no evidence of automatic exfiltration or an external callback controlled by the skill author.

low_findings.title: Environment variable references are configuration examples
low_findings.description: The process.env references configure Sentry release, environment, region, and instance tags. The surrounding example also removes cookies and authorization headers before sending events.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment values appear in a standard Sentry initialization example. The example includes explicit scrubbing, but users should still review telemetry payloads before production use.

low_findings.title: Diagnostic examples include user and session fields
low_findings.description: The structured logging example includes user ID, IP address, and session ID fields. This is common observability context, but users must redact or minimize personal data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly contains personal and session identifiers. The skill also tells users to redact secrets and PII, so this is a limited publication concern rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography and scheduling findings are scanner artifacts
low_findings.description: The cited weak-crypto locations are validation checks, retry logic, imports, or status constants. The at-command scheduling hit is ordinary incident timeline wording.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic algorithms or job scheduling commands. They are normal prose or language constructs inside examples.

content.value_statement: Application incidents are hard to diagnose when logs, traces, and symptoms are scattered. This skill provides a structured workflow for root-cause analysis, mitigation, and prevention.
actual_capabilities: Classifies errors by severity, type, observability, and user impact.
Guides stack trace review, log correlation, and distributed trace analysis.
Provides incident response phases from triage through postmortem review.
Suggests observability patterns for logging, metrics, tracing, and alerting.
Recommends validation steps, tests, fixes, and preventive action items.
limitations: Requires relevant logs, traces, error messages, or reproduction details.
Cannot verify production state without access to monitoring systems.
Does not replace human approval for production changes or rollbacks.
Example snippets must be adapted and reviewed before deployment.

use_cases.title: Triage a Production Incident
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to assess severity, gather observability data, form hypotheses, and plan mitigation.

use_cases.title: Investigate Recurring Exceptions
use_cases.description: Analyze stack traces, correlate logs, identify failure points, and define regression tests.

use_cases.title: Improve Error Observability
use_cases.description: Review logging, metrics, tracing, and alert patterns to improve detection and diagnosis.

prompt_templates.title: Explain This Error
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis of one error message
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this error message and explain the likely cause, severity, and next debugging steps: [paste error].

prompt_templates.title: Review a Stack Trace
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this stack trace. Identify the first application frame, likely invalid state, and tests that would confirm the fix: [paste trace].

prompt_templates.title: Triage an Incident
prompt_templates.prompt: Help triage this incident. Use these symptoms, metrics, logs, and recent changes to propose hypotheses and immediate mitigation: [details].

prompt_templates.title: Design a Prevention Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prevention plan for this root cause. Include observability improvements, code changes, tests, rollout steps, and ownership: [postmortem notes].

output_examples.input: A payment service has rising timeout errors after a new deployment.
output_examples.output: The skill would summarize impact, compare error timing with deployments, inspect dependency latency, propose rollback criteria, and list validation checks.

output_examples.input: A stack trace shows a null value passed into a framework collection method.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify the first application frame, trace how the value became null, and recommend validation and regression tests.

output_examples.input: A team wants better alerting for new error types.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend grouping by fingerprints, adding service tags, defining thresholds, and routing alerts to owners.

best_practices: Provide exact timestamps, service names, traces, logs, and recent deployment context.
Redact secrets, tokens, personal data, and customer identifiers before sharing diagnostics.
Validate each proposed fix with targeted tests and clear rollback steps.
anti_patterns: Do not paste unredacted secrets, session tokens, or production credentials into prompts.
Do not apply production changes without approval, monitoring, and rollback planning.
Do not treat example code snippets as deployment-ready implementation.

faq.question: What information should I provide first?
faq.answer: Provide the error message, stack trace, timestamps, affected service, recent changes, and observed user impact.

faq.question: Can this skill debug distributed systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for correlation IDs, service dependencies, traces, queues, and cascading failures.

faq.question: Does it make production changes automatically?
faq.answer: No. It recommends fixes and mitigation steps, but production changes require human approval.

faq.question: Can it help with observability design?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers structured logging, metrics, tracing, error tracking, and alerting patterns.

faq.question: Is it suitable for security incidents?
faq.answer: It can classify and investigate security-related errors, but it is not a full incident response framework.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
